sections.heading: 4: CBEST process
sections.summary: This section outlines the CBEST process framework for financial institutions and financial market infrastructures (FMIs) in the UK. It details the phased approach including initiation, threat intelligence, penetration testing, and closure phases, emphasizing intelligence-led security testing to identify vulnerabilities in critical business services. The process requires collaboration between regulators, firms, and third parties to ensure robust cyber resilience and timely remediation of identified weaknesses. This section details the CBEST (Cybersecurity Best Practices for Financial Services) process framework, requiring a Control Group Co-ordinator (CGC) to oversee implementation. The CGC must ensure essential expertise for CBEST execution, maintain risk management processes, and coordinate with Threat Intelligence Service Providers (TISP) and Penetration Test Service Providers (PTSP). The process spans four phases: Initiation, Threat Intelligence, Penetration Testing, and Closure, with specific deliverables and timelines for each stage. The CGC must also manage risks, ensure confidentiality, and maintain alignment with regulatory requirements throughout the assessment. This secti
sections.dtia_summary: This section describes the CBEST process as a regulatory framework for assessing cyber resilience, which is relevant for DTIAs as it outlines the need for structured security testing and vulnerability management across critical business services. It does not directly address data transfers but informs the broader cybersecurity context. This section establishes the CBEST process structure and risk management framework, which is critical for DTIAs as it mandates specific coordination mechanisms, risk controls, and confidentiality protocols when conducting cross-border cybersecurity assessments i

requirements.text: CBEST is a targeted assessment that allows regulators and firms to better understand weaknesses and vulnerabilities and take remedial actions, thereby improving the resilience of systemically important firms and by extension, the wider financial system.
Firms, FMIs or service providers can ask questions or provide feedback on the CBEST process to the PRA at: [email]
The regulator is responsible for using the deliverables from the CBEST assessment to form a view of the participant's cyber security posture.
The CBEST participant is the firm/FMI conducting the CBEST assessment.
The firm/FMI will need to select CG members, this is a team responsible for the management and firm oversight of the CBEST assessment.

CG must ensure that Control Group Co-ordinator (CGC) provides essential information and knowledge to implement CBEST (e.g., on IBSs, asset processes, etc) usually only one person per system, which is being tested as part of the CBEST

CG members should have authority to take relevant decisions, but membership is not necessarily limited to roles such as the Chief Operating Officer (COO), Chief Information Officer (CIO), Chief Technology Officer (CTO), Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
CG must ensure that all CBEST minimum criteria (Annex A – CBEST minimum criteria) and requirements described in the CBEST Implementation Guide are met during the implementation of the assessment
An overall project plan must be defined during the Initiation phase and systematically updated during the project
The TISP must be CBEST accredited (more details in Section 6.4.1)
The TISP will implement the TI analysis following the best practice described in the CBEST Services Assessment Guide (CBEST (2024b))

The TISP must complete the following tasks to satisfy the CBEST minimum criteria: the CBEST assessment is conducted in a risk-controlled manner, implementing a risk management process to identify, assess and mitigate risks related to CBEST activity throughout all phases

The TISP must ensure the secrecy of the CBEST assessment is preserved throughout its duration. If secrecy is compromised, or there is a suspicion that it has been, the CG must report this immediately to the regulator

The TISP must ensure the scope of the CBEST assessment is representative of the firm/FMI's IBSs. The key systems underpinning the IBSs and compromise actions in scope of CBEST are identified by mean of impact assessment
The TISP must ensure co-ordination, communication and engagement with and between all external parties (TISP, PTSP, regulator, etc) is effective
The TISP and PTSP engaged for the assessment must be accredited CBEST service providers (refer to Section 6.4.1)

Any significant concerns in relation to the project plan (e.g., delays) and the technical execution of Threat Intelligence (TI) and Penetration Testing (PT) phases must be reported to the regulator immediately
Deliverables must be produced in line with CBEST guidelines/templates and shared with the regulator on a timely and unredacted (unless otherwise required) basis

The TISP must provide an external threat intelligence assessment of the firm/FMI, which features evidentially supported profiles of cyber threat actors that could be reasonably expected to potentially target the firm/FMI
The TISP must provide information that potential threat actors could uncover about the IBSs and key systems identified as within the CBEST scope
The TISP must create threat scenarios based on the outcomes of the targeting assessment and threat intelligence
The PTSP must complete the Threat Intelligence Maturity Assessment of the firm/FMI's TI function based on the CBEST guidelines
The PTSP must provide further intelligence and direction during the PT phase and input to the final PT Report, as appropriate
The PTSP must ensure the TI analysis is aligned to the PT plan during the TI phase
The PTSP must design and plan the PT execution in line with the target actions agreed in the scope and the threat scenarios identified in the TI phase
The PTSP must agree a PT risk management process with the firm/FMI in order to run a controlled assessment and minimise the risks inherent in a CBEST assessment
The PTSP must execute the threat scenarios identified by the TISP and approved by the firm/FMI, using an ethical red teaming testing methodology
The PTSP must provide regular updates on the key target actions implemented and the results during the PT phase
The PTSP must complete the Detection & Response (D&R) Capability Assessment (CBEST (2024i)) of the firm/FMI based on the CBEST guidelines
The PTSP must draft the PT Findings Report in line with the CBEST guidelines
The PTSP must provide feedback on the CBEST execution during the Debrief session with the regulator
During Scoping, the regulator will check if the firm/FMI is registered to the NCSC Early Warning Service (EWS) and ask for confirmation that the firm/FMI's data are up to date in the NCSC system

The CG must ensure the CBEST assessment is conducted in a risk-controlled manner, implementing a risk management process to identify, assess and mitigate risks related to CBEST activity throughout all phases
The CG must identify, analyse, and manage the risks that could negatively impact the CBEST assessment
The CG must plan and implement mitigating actions for each risk identified
The CG must ensure the risks are reduced through advanced planning, clear definition of the scope and predefined escalation procedures
The CG must look to ensure the risks are reduced through advanced planning, clear definition of the scope and predefined escalation procedures
The CG must remain in control of CBEST for the whole implementation of the assessment
The CG can order a temporary halt if concerns are raised over damage (or potential damage) to a system or disruption to an IBS
The CG must complete a dedicated CBEST risk assessment prior to the CBEST commencing
The CG must regularly review the identified mitigating measures to ensure they remain appropriate throughout the process
The CG must assign a project code name (unrelated to the organisation's name) for referencing the organisation within all CBEST communications and documentation
CBEST deliverables must be managed during their lifecycle to ensure sensitive information is removed before sharing with the regulator

The CG must ensure that Personally Identifiable Information and technical details (such as Internet Protocols, system names, emails, configuration details, etc) are removed from the reports before sharing with the regulator
The CG must remove Personally Identifiable Information and technical details (such as Internet Protocols, system names, emails, configuration details) from reports before sharing with the regulator.
The CG must ensure deliverables shared with the regulator do not contain sensitive information unnecessary for regulatory analysis.
CBEST activities must be executed on live-production systems unless legal or ethical constraints exist.
The CG must complete a risk assessment identifying and evaluating risks affecting CBEST.
The CG must prepare a Privacy Impact Document (PID) covering CBEST governance and risk management arrangements.
The firm/FMI must sign off on a final Scope Specification for delivery to the regulator.
The CG must ensure third-party members operate within the same security protocols and confidentiality limitations.
The regulator and CG must formalize the document (CBEST (2024d)) and agree on compromise actions for testers.
All compromise actions agreed in the document must be prioritized in the Penetration Test Plan.
The TISP must produce an IBS-focused Threat Intelligence Plan that is delivered to the firm/FMI and PTSP.
The TI plan must allow time for deliverable reviews and workshops and make explicit key deliverable handover points.
If the TISP identifies a major vulnerability or imminent threat that could compromise a scoped IBS, it must disclose this immediately to the CG.
The CG must remediate any vulnerabilities identified and discuss remediated vulnerabilities with the PTSP to avoid being at a disadvantage during penetration testing.
The TISP must collect, analyse and disseminate IBS-focused intelligence on relevant threat actors and probable threat scenarios.
The TISP must develop scenarios based on threat intelligence that map to one or more IBS-supporting systems.
The CG and PTSP should agree on the best setup, including account profiles and privilege access levels.
The CG and PTSP should agree on relevant business or operational information needed to simulate scenarios.
The CG should plan the involvement of staff and third parties to increase the realism of the assessment.
The CG and PTSP should consider involving staff/third parties and using real user profiles and live devices to reduce detection risk by the SOC.
Advanced simulations of malicious insiders or supply chain scenarios require detailed planning ahead of execution.
The CG may agree to allow the PTSP to test up to a specific point without causing actual damage.
Firms should consider exploring scenarios beyond the CBEST assessment scope for future follow-up testing.
Sufficient time must be budgeted to make the PT as realistic as possible.
The assessment must cover the end-to-end processes and systems supporting the in-scope IBS, unless otherwise agreed between all parties.
PTSPs must collaborate with the firm/FMI in refining the PT plan to execute all in-scope scenarios.
If scenarios are not fully implemented, an extension of the exercise should be considered.
The CG prepare an initial draft of the Remediation Plan, using the CBEST Remediation Plan Template (CBEST (2024j)) provided by the regulator

The CG should review and draw upon the evidence, observation and technical findings, as well as the recommendations and remediation proposed by the TISP and PTSP in the Targeting Report, the Threat Intelligence Report and the final Penetration Test Report, respectively
The draft Remediation Plan should also consider improvement actions in the weakest areas identified in the TIMA and the D&R assessment reports
The CG and the regulator meet to review the outcome of the assessment and the Remediation Plan prepared by the CG

The final Remediation Plan should capture the risk and impact assessments completed by the firm/FMI with regards to the technical findings from all CBEST phases and how these translate to business risk and the IBSs

The final Remediation Plan should cover the governance around the Remediation Plan, input from the Board, senior management, risk owners and risk management functions, and the technical activities agreed with technical leaders and subject matter experts both for tactical and strategic remediation
The findings and lessons learned from CBEST should be used to inform read-across assessments for areas not in scope of CBEST

sections.source_quote: CBEST is a targeted assessment that allows regulators and firms to better understand weaknesses and vulnerabilities and take remedial actions, thereby improving the resilience of systemically important firms and by extension, the wider financial system.
